title: Clock Skew and Server Ordering
description: Two devices with clocks 2 hours apart creating conflicting custody schedules for the same child with the same effectiveFrom. Demonstrates that globalSequence (server ordering) determines application order, while clientTimestamp is only used for tie-breaking within conflict resolution. Even though Device B's clock is 2 hours ahead, the conflict resolution result is deterministic.

deviceB.name: Bob's Phone
deviceB.clockOffset: +2 hours AHEAD of actual UTC (clock skew)

scenario.step1: Both devices go offline around the same real-world moment.
scenario.step2: Device A creates a custody schedule. Its clientTimestamp is 2026-03-28T10:00:00.000Z (accurate).
scenario.step3: Device B creates a competing custody schedule seconds later in real time. Its clientTimestamp is 2026-03-28T12:00:30.000Z (2h ahead + 30s real elapsed).
scenario.step4: Device B comes online first and uploads. Server assigns globalSequence=1.
scenario.step5: Device A comes online second and uploads. Server assigns globalSequence=2.
scenario.step6: Key insight: Device B uploaded first (got lower globalSequence) but Device A has the earlier clientTimestamp.

deviceBOp.description: Device B creates schedule (uploads FIRST, gets lower globalSequence)

deviceAOp.description: Device A creates schedule (uploads SECOND, gets higher globalSequence)

applicationOrder.description: Clients MUST apply ops in globalSequence order, regardless of clientTimestamp

order.action: Apply Device B's schedule. It becomes the active schedule for the child.
Apply Device A's schedule. Same effectiveFrom triggers conflict resolution.

step2.caveat: Device B's clock was 2 hours ahead, giving it an artificially later timestamp. The protocol accepts this because clientTimestamp is only a tie-breaker, not a source of truth for ordering.

keyInsights.detail: Device B's op (globalSeq=1) is applied BEFORE Device A's op (globalSeq=2), even though Device B's clientTimestamp (12:00:30) is later than Device A's (10:00:00). The server arrival order determines when ops are processed.

keyInsights.insight: clientTimestamp is ONLY for tie-breaking within conflict resolution
keyInsights.detail: When two schedules have the same effectiveFrom, clientTimestamp breaks the tie. It does NOT affect the order in which ops are applied to the local database.

keyInsights.insight: Clock skew can influence conflict resolution outcomes
keyInsights.detail: A device with a clock running ahead gets an advantage in timestamp-based tie-breaks. This is a known and accepted tradeoff. The protocol recommends NTP synchronization and warns users about clock skew exceeding 5 minutes.
serverTimestamp (10:01:00 for Device B, 10:02:00 for Device A) shows the real arrival order, but it is NOT used in conflict resolution. Only clientTimestamp is used for tie-breaking.
Despite the clock skew, both devices arrive at the same final state after applying ops in globalSequence order. The algorithm is deterministic regardless of which device processes first.

activeSchedule.reason: Won conflict resolution via later clientTimestamp (even though clock was skewed)

supersededSchedule.reason: Lost conflict resolution: same effectiveFrom, earlier clientTimestamp
